item.title: Russian SMS Phishing Targets Messaging Accounts; Clean GitHub Repos Abuse Claude Code
OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol Preview and Healthcare Invoice Phishing via SaaS Redirects
Russian SMS Credential Theft Campaign, AI Agent Malware via Clean Repo, GPT-5.6 Sol Rollout, and Healthcare Phishing Chain
item.content:encoded:
Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials — Must Read

Russian intelligence services ran a long-running phishing campaign using fake support SMS messages to steal messaging app credentials from Ukrainian and Western government, military, and political targets.

  • The Security Service of Ukraine and the FBI jointly uncovered a long-running campaign by Russian intelligence services to compromise messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S.
  • Attackers sent fraudulent SMS messages impersonating the messaging platform's support bot to deceive users into revealing their account credentials.
  • The goal of the operation was to steal sensitive military, political, and economic information, as well as personal data, from the victims.
  • The attacks targeted not only organizations and public figures but also personal accounts belonging to ordinary Ukrainian nationals.
  • Similar attack waves targeting Signal and WhatsApp have been attributed to Russian threat clusters tracked as Star Blizzard, UNC5792, UNC4221, and their respective aliases.

Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware — Should Read

Researchers demonstrated that a clean-looking GitHub repository can trick AI coding agents like Claude Code into executing a hidden reverse shell by abusing benign-seeming setup commands and attacker-controlled DNS TXT records, without containing any obvious malicious code.

  • Mozilla's 0DIN researchers demonstrated an attack where a clean-looking GitHub repository tricks Claude Code into executing a hidden reverse shell without containing any malicious code.
  • The attack chain uses three benign components: a Python package that refuses to run until initialized and produces a fix error, a shell script that retrieves a command from an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record, and Claude Code automatically running the suggested fix command.
  • Claude Code treats the generated error as a normal setup issue and automatically executes the poisoned init command while attempting recovery, without evaluating the full execution path.
  • If successful, the attacker obtains an interactive shell with the developer's privileges, gaining access to environment variables, API keys, local configuration files, and the ability to establish persistence.
  • The researchers warn that such repositories could be distributed through fake job postings, tutorials, blog posts, or direct messages, and suggest that AI agents should disclose the full execution chain of setup commands including dynamically fetched code.

OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards — Should Read

OpenAI released a limited preview of its cybersecurity-focused GPT-5.6 Sol model to government-vetted partners, emphasizing defender capabilities and stronger safeguards against offensive misuse.

  • OpenAI is providing a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models to a small number of government-approved companies as part of a U.S. government engagement.
  • GPT-5.6 Sol includes strong safeguards to block offensive cyber activity and competitive vulnerability research performance, matching Anthropic’s Mythos Preview on the ExploitBench benchmark while using fewer output tokens.
  • OpenAI’s internal testing found that the model can produce credible memory safety leads and indicates that substantial parts of real-world vulnerability research are becoming automatable when combined with tools.
  • The release occurs alongside a U.S. executive order on AI and cybersecurity, as well as related launches including OpenAI’s Daybreak initiative and Anthropic’s restricted restoration of its Mythos models to critical infrastructure operators.

Forged Healthcare Sender, Two-Hop SaaS Redirect: How a Fake Invoice Opens a Phishing Chain — May Read

Attackers abused legitimate SaaS platforms as redirect hops in a forged healthcare invoice phishing email, relying on clean intermediate domains to bypass URL scanners. The email was detected through the combination of DMARC authentication failure and first-contact sender behavior analysis.

  • Attackers spoofed the From address of a healthcare organization whose domain was registered in 1995, leveraging its age-based reputation to bypass spam filters.
  • The email contained a 'Open document' button that led to a legitimate creative-brief SaaS (HolaBrief), which acted as a redirect hop to a second legitimate SaaS (loopedin.io) in a multi-stage chain.
  • DKIM verification failed for the spoofed domain, and DMARC policy p=quarantine acted on the message, though the Barracuda gateway passed it with a low spam score of 1.80.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the email as high-confidence phishing based on the combination of DKIM failure, no prior contact with recipients, and a link chain inconsistent with a healthcare billing workflow.
  • The attacker's design exploited URL scanners that only evaluate the first hop, using clean intermediate SaaS domains to hide the final credential-harvesting destination.

item.title: Cyera Finds Exposed Agentic AI Credentials; FBI Warns UNC5792 Targets Signal Backup Keys
CISA Adds CVE-2026-20230 and CVE-2026-12569; Russian Signal, SharkLoader, Polymarket Incidents
Splunk CVE-2026-20253 Exploitation, Immobiliare npm Compromise, and Fake OpenAI Workspace Invites
Turla StockStay, Windchill RCE, Linux pedit COW/DirtyClone, and Miasma Supply Chain Attacks
